title: A Seminar on Moral Luck
summary: A philosophy professor and a graduate student explore the concept of moral luck — the extent to which factors beyond our control influence moral judgements about persons — examining its implications for responsibility, blame, desert, and whether a coherent account of moral agency is possible.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: Let's begin with Thomas Nagel's original framing of the problem. Nagel observes that we make moral judgements about people that are deeply affected by factors entirely outside their control — what he calls 'moral luck'. The drunk driver who reaches home without incident is morally assessed very differently from the identical drunk driver whose car strikes a child who runs into the road. The external difference — the presence or absence of the child — determines the moral verdict, despite the fact that the two drivers' intentions, character, and choices were identical up to that point. What troubles Nagel is that this seems both inevitable and indefensible.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: It's indefensible, presumably, because it violates what we might call the control principle — the intuition that people can only be legitimately held responsible for what is within their control. If the outcome depended on something the agent couldn't have altered — the random appearance of a child — then holding the unlucky driver more responsible than the lucky one seems to punish people for luck, which violates our intuitions about fairness. And yet you say it's also inevitable. Why?

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: Because if we take the control principle seriously and try to strip away all factors outside the agent's control, we find that we have stripped away too much. Not only outcomes but circumstances, character, and even the causal chains that produced the agent's own decisions are, to varying degrees, outside their control. Nagel distinguishes four types: resultant luck — luck in how things turn out; circumstantial luck — luck in the situations one faces; constitutive luck — luck in who one is, one's temperament and character; and causal luck — luck in how one is determined by antecedent causes. Applied consistently, the control principle would dissolve moral responsibility altogether.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: That's a genuinely vertiginous conclusion. It seems to undermine not just our practices of praise and blame but the entire architecture of moral life. If I cannot take credit for virtues I did not choose to have, and cannot be blamed for vices that resulted from circumstances I did not choose, then the concept of moral responsibility becomes either empty or incoherent. And yet we can't simply abandon it. The reactive attitudes — resentment, indignation, gratitude — that Strawson identified as constitutive of moral relationships seem to presuppose it.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: That's precisely the tension Nagel is trying to articulate — and I don't think he resolves it. He concludes that the problem of moral luck reveals a genuine contradiction at the heart of our moral thinking: we simultaneously hold two commitments that cannot be fully reconciled. We are committed to the control principle as a condition of fairness, and we are committed to the practice of holding people responsible in ways that outrun their control. Living with this tension is, perhaps, all we can do.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: Susan Wolf offers a different response, doesn't she? Rather than accepting the tension as irresolvable, she attempts to identify the conditions under which responsibility is genuinely appropriate — a 'reason-responsiveness' account, if I recall correctly.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: Yes, though the reason-responsiveness account is more commonly associated with John Martin Fischer and Mark Ravizza. Wolf's contribution is the 'ability to do otherwise' plus a sanity condition — roughly, the idea that a responsible agent must be able both to grasp and apply reasons and to have the kind of psychological integrity that allows self-authorship. On this account, what matters is not whether the agent controlled every causal factor in their history but whether, at the moment of action, they had the relevant capacities — to recognise reasons, to respond to them, and to act accordingly. Is that a satisfying resolution to you?

body.speaker: Student
body.text: It's partially satisfying. It does carve out a space for genuine responsibility by focusing on the agent's capacities at the moment of action rather than on the uncontrollable antecedents of those capacities. But it seems to me that it doesn't fully address constitutive luck. Even the capacities the agent brings to the moment of decision — the ability to reason well, the sensitivity to moral considerations, the psychological stability — are themselves shaped by factors beyond their control. One person's capacity for moral reasoning was cultivated by stable, loving parents; another's was stunted by neglect or abuse. Both exercise whatever capacities they have, but those capacities are radically unequal, and that inequality is not their fault.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: A powerful objection, and one that Derk Pereboom takes seriously in his case for hard incompatibilism. Pereboom argues that, given our best understanding of the causal structure of the universe — whether deterministic or indeterministic — no one is ever truly morally responsible in the basic desert sense. We may still have good reasons for practices of praise, blame, and punishment — forward-looking reasons related to consequences and rehabilitation — but the retributive element, the idea that wrongdoers basically deserve to suffer, cannot be justified. Do you find that conclusion liberating or alarming?

body.speaker: Student
body.text: Both, I think. Liberating, because hard incompatibilism offers the most principled basis for a compassionate system of criminal justice — one that rejects punishment as retribution and focuses on harm-reduction, rehabilitation, and social protection. Alarming, because it seems to erode the self-conception that underpins human dignity: the sense that I am the author of my own life, that my choices are genuinely mine, that what I make of myself reflects something real about me. If constitutive luck goes all the way down, then even my response to this seminar — my engagement, my arguments — is ultimately a product of factors I did not choose.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: And that, I think, is where the practical philosophy intersects with the existential. The most we can say, perhaps, is what Gary Watson has suggested: that responsibility is not a metaphysical property we either have or lack entirely, but a gradient that varies with the degree to which an agent's action expresses their evaluative commitments — their deepest concerns and values — regardless of how those commitments were formed. We hold people responsible, on this view, not because they could have done otherwise in some absolute sense, but because their action expressed something about who they are. And who they are, however formed, is genuinely them.

annotations.meaningVi: vận may đạo đức — khi phán xét đạo đức bị ảnh hưởng bởi các yếu tố ngoài tầm kiểm soát của người đó
annotations.exampleEn: Moral luck operates when two people who behaved identically are judged differently because of different outcomes.

annotations.meaningVi: nguyên tắc kiểm soát — chỉ có thể quy trách nhiệm đạo đức cho những gì trong tầm kiểm soát của người đó
annotations.exampleEn: The control principle implies that we cannot blame someone for what they could not have altered.

annotations.meaningVi: vận may kết quả — may mắn trong cách mọi thứ diễn ra
annotations.exampleEn: Resultant luck determines whether an attempt at harm succeeds or fails.

annotations.meaningVi: vận may cấu thành — may mắn trong việc là người như thế nào, tính cách và khí chất
annotations.exampleEn: Constitutive luck refers to being born with a certain temperament or having capacities shaped by circumstances.

annotations.meaningVi: vận may nhân quả — may mắn trong cách người ta bị quyết định bởi nguyên nhân tiền đề
annotations.exampleEn: If determinism is true, all our choices result from causal luck going back to factors before our birth.

annotations.meaningVi: thái độ phản ứng — cảm xúc như oán giận, phẫn nộ, biết ơn phụ thuộc vào việc coi người khác có trách nhiệm
annotations.exampleEn: Strawson argued that reactive attitudes like resentment and gratitude are central to moral relationships.

annotations.meaningVi: chủ nghĩa bất tương thích cứng — quan điểm rằng không ai có trách nhiệm đạo đức thực sự dù có hay không có thuyết tất định
annotations.exampleEn: Hard incompatibilism holds that moral responsibility in the desert sense is impossible regardless of whether the universe is deterministic.

annotations.meaningVi: yếu tố trả thù — quan điểm rằng kẻ phạm tội đáng bị trừng phạt vì họ xứng đáng chịu
annotations.exampleEn: The retributive element of punishment holds that suffering is deserved, not merely useful for deterrence.

annotations.meaningVi: khả năng đáp ứng lý do — khả năng nhận ra và đáp lại các lý do hành động
annotations.exampleEn: A reason-responsive agent can modify their behaviour when given compelling reasons to do so.

annotations.meaningVi: quyền tự tác giả hoá cuộc sống — cảm giác là tác giả của cuộc đời mình
annotations.exampleEn: Self-authorship requires the psychological integrity to make genuine choices that express who you are.

annotations.meaningVi: nguyên nhân tiền đề — các nguyên nhân đi trước và quyết định hành động hiện tại
annotations.exampleEn: Causal luck refers to how our choices are shaped by antecedent causes we did not choose.

annotations.meaningVi: kết luận gây chóng mặt — nhận định gây cảm giác mất thăng bằng hoặc bất an
annotations.exampleEn: The vertiginous conclusion that no one is ever responsible dissolves the basis of our legal and moral systems.

annotations.meaningVi: vận may hoàn cảnh — may mắn trong các tình huống mà người ta phải đối mặt
annotations.exampleEn: Whether one was born in a time of war or peace is a matter of circumstantial luck.

annotations.meaningVi: cam kết đánh giá — các giá trị và mối quan tâm sâu sắc nhất của một người
annotations.exampleEn: Gary Watson argues that responsibility is expressed through an agent's evaluative commitments — what they care most about.

annotations.meaningVi: nghĩa đáng về cơ bản — cảm giác rằng kẻ phạm tội xứng đáng chịu trừng phạt tự thân nó
annotations.exampleEn: Pereboom denies moral responsibility in the basic desert sense — no one truly deserves to suffer for what they did.

annotations.meaningVi: điều kiện lý trí — yêu cầu người chịu trách nhiệm phải có khả năng nhận thức đúng đắn
annotations.exampleEn: Susan Wolf's sanity condition requires that a responsible agent can both understand and apply moral reasons.

annotations.meaningVi: không nhất quán, thiếu mạch lạc, mâu thuẫn
annotations.exampleEn: A theory that requires both free will and determinism is internally incoherent.

annotations.meaningVi: xoá bỏ trách nhiệm đạo đức — làm cho khái niệm trách nhiệm không còn áp dụng được
annotations.exampleEn: A strict application of the control principle would dissolve moral responsibility entirely.

annotations.meaningVi: làm nền tảng cho, hỗ trợ từ bên dưới
annotations.exampleEn: The belief in free will underpins our entire system of criminal justice.

annotations.meaningVi: thang độ, độ dốc — sự thay đổi dần dần thay vì phân chia nhị phân
annotations.exampleEn: Watson views responsibility not as binary but as a gradient — more or less, not all or nothing.

grammarNotes.title: Embedded Concessive Clauses: 'despite the fact that', 'regardless of how'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mệnh đề nhượng bộ nhúng vào (embedded concessive clauses) thừa nhận một sự thật phụ trong khi duy trì lập luận chính. 'The two drivers' intentions, character, and choices were identical up to that point' — nhúng vào 'despite the fact that the two drivers' intentions... were identical.' 'Regardless of how those commitments were formed' nhúng nhượng bộ vào cụm danh từ. Cấu trúc: 'despite the fact that + mệnh đề' / 'regardless of how + mệnh đề' / 'however formed' (dạng rút gọn). Đây là công cụ thiết yếu trong triết học để thừa nhận sự thật phức tạp mà không làm suy yếu lập luận.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Despite the fact that the two drivers' intentions were identical' is a full embedded concessive clause. Shorter forms include 'regardless of how those commitments were formed' and 'however formed, is genuinely them.' These constructions allow a writer to make a concession without abandoning the main claim. 'Despite the fact that X' is more explicit than 'though X' — use it when the conceded point is important enough to flag clearly. In philosophical argument, embedded concessives show you are aware of counterexamples without treating them as decisive.

grammarNotes.title: Appositive Dashes for Technical Definition: '— luck in how things turn out'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn triết học và học thuật, dấu gạch ngang (em dash) thường được dùng để định nghĩa một khái niệm kỹ thuật ngay sau khi nó được giới thiệu. 'Resultant luck — luck in how things turn out; circumstantial luck — luck in the situations one faces; constitutive luck — luck in who one is.' Cấu trúc: [thuật ngữ] — [định nghĩa ngắn]. Dấu chấm phẩy (;) phân tách các mục trong danh sách khi mỗi mục đã có dấu phẩy hoặc dấu gạch ngang bên trong. Kỹ thuật này cho phép xây dựng thuật ngữ học hiệu quả trong văn xuôi, không cần chú thích hay danh sách riêng biệt.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dash-definition pattern — '[term] — [brief gloss]' — is efficient for introducing technical vocabulary within flowing prose. Nagel's four types use this pattern consistently: 'resultant luck — luck in how things turn out; circumstantial luck — luck in the situations one faces...' The semicolons separate the four types since each contains an internal dash. This pattern is common in philosophical writing because it avoids the interruption of footnotes and keeps the argument moving while still introducing necessary terms. At C1-C2, mastering this pattern allows you to build specialised vocabulary smoothly.

translationVi:
Giáo sư giới thiệu khung của Thomas Nagel về vận may đạo đức: hai tài xế say rượu đồng nhất về ý định và hành vi nhưng được đánh giá đạo đức rất khác nhau dựa trên kết quả ngẫu nhiên — sự có hay vắng mặt của một đứa trẻ. Nagel gọi điều này vừa là không thể bào chữa (vi phạm nguyên tắc kiểm soát) vừa là không thể tránh (vì áp dụng nhất quán nguyên tắc kiểm soát sẽ xoá bỏ hoàn toàn trách nhiệm đạo đức).

Nagel phân biệt bốn loại vận may: kết quả, hoàn cảnh, cấu thành và nhân quả. Sinh viên nhận ra rằng điều này đe doạ toàn bộ kiến trúc của đời sống đạo đức. Giáo sư xác nhận Nagel không giải quyết được mâu thuẫn — sống với căng thẳng này là tất cả những gì chúng ta có thể làm.

Sinh viên nhắc đến Susan Wolf và lý thuyết đáp ứng lý do. Giáo sư làm rõ và giải thích đóng góp của Wolf, nhưng sinh viên phản bác rằng nó không giải quyết được vận may cấu thành — các năng lực mà người đại diện mang đến khoảnh khắc quyết định đều được hình thành bởi các yếu tố ngoài tầm kiểm soát của họ.

Giáo sư đưa ra Pereboom và chủ nghĩa bất tương thích cứng — không ai thực sự có trách nhiệm đạo đức theo nghĩa đáng cơ bản. Sinh viên thấy điều này vừa giải phóng vừa đáng lo ngại. Giáo sư kết thúc với quan điểm của Gary Watson: trách nhiệm là thang độ, không phải thuộc tính nhị phân, biến đổi theo mức độ hành động của người đại diện thể hiện các cam kết đánh giá của họ — điều đó, dù được hình thành như thế nào, là thực sự của họ.
criticalThinkingQuestion: The professor concludes with Watson's view that an agent's action 'expressed something about who they are' — and that 'who they are, however formed, is genuinely them.' Is this a satisfying response to the problem of constitutive luck, or does it simply relocate the problem? If who you are was shaped by factors you did not choose, in what sense is it 'genuinely you' rather than genuinely luck?

questions.prompt: What is the example Nagel uses to illustrate moral luck, as described by the professor?
options: A surgeon who makes a rare error that causes permanent harm to a patient
Two drunk drivers with identical intentions and behaviour, one of whom hits a child who runs into the road
A soldier who obeys an illegal order versus one who refuses but has the same moral character
A wealthy investor who profits from a market crash they did not foresee versus one who loses everything
questions.explanation: 'The drunk driver who reaches home without incident is morally assessed very differently from the identical drunk driver whose car strikes a child who runs into the road.'
questions.hint: The example involves two people who are identical in all controllable respects but whose outcomes differ.

questions.prompt: What does the 'control principle' state, according to the student?
options: Moral judgements should be based only on the consequences of an action, not on intentions
People can only be legitimately held responsible for what is within their control
Responsibility is inversely proportional to the degree of situational pressure a person faces
Legal responsibility and moral responsibility should always coincide
questions.explanation: 'the control principle — the intuition that people can only be legitimately held responsible for what is within their control.'
questions.hint: The student defines this principle in relation to what it says about legitimate responsibility.

questions.prompt: What does Nagel identify as 'constitutive luck'?
options: Luck in how one's actions turn out — whether a plan succeeds or fails
Luck in the situations one happens to face during one's lifetime
Luck in who one is — one's temperament, character, and psychological dispositions
Luck in how one's actions are causally determined by prior events
questions.explanation: 'constitutive luck — luck in who one is, one's temperament and character'
questions.hint: Of the four types, constitutive luck concerns identity and character, not outcomes or circumstances.

questions.prompt: What is Nagel's conclusion about the tension between the control principle and actual moral practice?
options: The control principle should be abandoned in favour of consequentialist assessment
Both commitments can be reconciled through a careful analysis of degrees of control
The two commitments cannot be fully reconciled, and living with this tension is all we can do
The tension proves that moral responsibility is an illusion that should be abandoned
questions.explanation: 'He concludes that the problem of moral luck reveals a genuine contradiction at the heart of our moral thinking... Living with this tension is, perhaps, all we can do.'
questions.hint: Does Nagel resolve the tension or conclude something else?

questions.prompt: What are the 'reactive attitudes' that Strawson identified as constitutive of moral relationships?
options: Shame, pride, and moral satisfaction in response to one's own actions
Resentment, indignation, and gratitude in response to how others act
Fear, hope, and desire in anticipation of future events
Sympathy, empathy, and compassion in response to others' suffering
questions.explanation: 'The reactive attitudes — resentment, indignation, gratitude — that Strawson identified as constitutive of moral relationships seem to presuppose it.'
questions.hint: The three examples given are responses to how other people treat us.

questions.prompt: Who is more commonly associated with the 'reason-responsiveness' account of moral responsibility, according to the professor?
options: John Martin Fischer and Mark Ravizza
questions.explanation: 'the reason-responsiveness account is more commonly associated with John Martin Fischer and Mark Ravizza.'
questions.hint: The professor corrects the student's attribution to Wolf and names two other philosophers.

questions.prompt: What is Susan Wolf's 'sanity condition' for moral responsibility?
options: The agent must be free from mental illness at the time of the act
The agent must be able to grasp and apply reasons and have the psychological integrity for self-authorship
The agent must be aware that their action is morally wrong before committing it
The agent must have the capacity to feel remorse after the act
questions.explanation: 'a responsible agent must be able both to grasp and apply reasons and to have the kind of psychological integrity that allows self-authorship.'
questions.hint: Wolf's condition has two components: what must the agent be able to do with reasons, and what kind of integrity must they have?

questions.prompt: What is the student's objection to the reason-responsiveness account?
options: It fails to explain why we punish people who lack free will
It doesn't address constitutive luck — the capacities brought to a decision are themselves shaped by uncontrollable factors
It applies only to rational agents and excludes those with diminished capacity
It requires too high a standard of reason-recognition that few people can actually meet
questions.explanation: 'it doesn't fully address constitutive luck. Even the capacities the agent brings to the moment of decision... are themselves shaped by factors beyond their control.'
questions.hint: Even if we focus on the moment of action, what about the capacities the agent has at that moment?

questions.prompt: What does Pereboom's hard incompatibilism conclude about moral responsibility?
options: Moral responsibility is possible only in a deterministic universe
No one is ever truly morally responsible in the basic desert sense, regardless of whether determinism is true
Moral responsibility is real but cannot be measured precisely enough to justify punishment
Moral responsibility applies only to premeditated actions, not impulsive ones
questions.explanation: 'Pereboom argues that... no one is ever truly morally responsible in the basic desert sense.'
questions.hint: Pereboom's view applies regardless of whether the universe is deterministic or not.

questions.prompt: What does the student find 'liberating' about hard incompatibilism?
options: It eliminates the anxiety of feeling personally responsible for all one's failures
It offers the most principled basis for a compassionate criminal justice system that rejects retribution
It allows individuals to feel genuinely free since determinism is disproved
It removes the social stigma attached to mental illness and criminal behaviour
questions.explanation: 'Liberating, because hard incompatibilism offers the most principled basis for a compassionate system of criminal justice — one that rejects punishment as retribution and focuses on harm-reduction, rehabilitation, and social protection.'
questions.hint: What type of justice system does hard incompatibilism provide the best basis for?

questions.prompt: What does the student find 'alarming' about hard incompatibilism?
options: It implies that dangerous criminals cannot be lawfully detained for public safety reasons
It erodes the self-conception underpinning human dignity — the sense of being the genuine author of one's life
It makes moral education and character development pointless
It eliminates the distinction between premeditated and accidental harm
questions.explanation: 'Alarming, because it seems to erode the self-conception that underpins human dignity: the sense that I am the author of my own life, that my choices are genuinely mine.'
questions.hint: What aspect of self-understanding does hard incompatibilism threaten?

questions.prompt: What is Gary Watson's view of moral responsibility, as described by the professor?
options: Responsibility is a binary property — agents either have it fully or lack it entirely
Responsibility varies with the degree to which an agent's action expresses their deepest evaluative commitments
Responsibility is determined entirely by the agent's ability to have done otherwise
Responsibility is a social construct with no metaphysical basis
questions.explanation: 'responsibility is not a metaphysical property we either have or lack entirely, but a gradient that varies with the degree to which an agent's action expresses their evaluative commitments — their deepest concerns and values.'
questions.hint: Watson views responsibility as a gradient — not binary. What does the gradient measure?

questions.prompt: How does the professor characterise the significance of 'who they are, however formed, is genuinely them'?
options: It proves that constitutive luck is not morally relevant because identity is always authentic
It provides a basis for holding people responsible through their action's expression of their genuine identity, even if that identity was formed by luck
It shows that character traits are more important than intentions in moral assessment
It establishes that free will is compatible with determinism because identity is self-generated
questions.explanation: Watson's point is that we hold people responsible because their action expressed who they genuinely are — and their identity, however formed, is authentically theirs, providing a basis for moral assessment even without absolute control.
questions.hint: How does Watson's view ground responsibility despite acknowledging that identity is shaped by luck?

questions.prompt: What does the professor say Nagel's problem of moral luck 'reveals' about our moral thinking?
options: That moral intuitions are culturally relative and cannot provide universal guidance
A genuine contradiction — two commitments to fairness and responsibility that cannot be fully reconciled
That consequentialist ethics is ultimately more coherent than deontological alternatives
That the concept of blame should be abandoned in favour of forward-looking practices
questions.explanation: 'the problem of moral luck reveals a genuine contradiction at the heart of our moral thinking: we simultaneously hold two commitments that cannot be fully reconciled.'
questions.hint: What specific structure does the problem reveal — a limitation, a contradiction, or an error?

questions.prompt: What is the 'retributive element' that Pereboom says cannot be justified?
options: The practice of publicising criminal sentences to deter others
The idea that wrongdoers basically deserve to suffer as punishment for what they did
The use of imprisonment to separate dangerous individuals from society
The requirement that offenders pay compensation to their victims
questions.explanation: 'the retributive element, the idea that wrongdoers basically deserve to suffer, cannot be justified.'
questions.hint: Retribution is about desert — what wrongdoers deserve — not about consequences or social protection.

cloze.template:
Professor: Let's begin with Thomas Nagel's original framing of the problem. Nagel observes that we make moral judgements about people that are deeply affected by factors entirely outside their control — what he calls '{{b1}}'. The drunk driver who reaches home without incident is morally assessed very differently from the identical drunk driver whose car strikes a child who runs into the road. The external difference — the presence or absence of the child — determines the moral verdict, despite the fact that the two drivers' intentions, character, and choices were identical up to that point. What troubles Nagel is that this seems both inevitable and indefensible.
Student: It's indefensible, presumably, because it violates what we might call the {{b2}} principle — the intuition that people can only be legitimately held responsible for what is within their control. If the outcome depended on something the agent couldn't have altered — the random appearance of a child — then holding the unlucky driver more responsible than the lucky one seems to punish people for luck, which violates our intuitions about fairness.
Professor: Because if we take the control principle seriously and try to strip away all factors outside the agent's control, we find that we have stripped away too much. Nagel distinguishes four types: resultant luck — luck in how things turn out; {{b3}} luck — luck in the situations one faces; {{b4}} luck — luck in who one is, one's temperament and character; and causal luck — luck in how one is determined by antecedent causes. Applied consistently, the control principle would {{b5}} moral responsibility altogether.
Student: That's a genuinely {{b6}} conclusion. It seems to undermine not just our practices of praise and blame but the entire architecture of moral life. If I cannot take credit for virtues I did not choose to have, and cannot be blamed for vices that resulted from circumstances I did not choose, then the concept of moral responsibility becomes either empty or incoherent. And yet we can't simply abandon it. The {{b7}} attitudes — resentment, indignation, gratitude — that Strawson identified as constitutive of moral relationships seem to presuppose it.
Professor: He concludes that the problem of moral luck reveals a genuine contradiction at the heart of our moral thinking: we simultaneously hold two commitments that cannot be fully reconciled. We are committed to the control principle as a condition of fairness, and we are committed to the practice of holding people responsible in ways that outrun their control. Living with this tension is, perhaps, all we can do.
Professor: Wolf's contribution is the 'ability to do otherwise' plus a {{b8}} condition — roughly, the idea that a responsible agent must be able both to grasp and apply reasons and to have the kind of psychological integrity that allows {{b9}}. On this account, what matters is not whether the agent controlled every causal factor in their history but whether, at the moment of action, they had the relevant capacities — to recognise reasons, to respond to them, and to act accordingly.
Student: It's partially satisfying. It does carve out a space for genuine responsibility by focusing on the agent's capacities at the moment of action rather than on the uncontrollable {{b10}} of those capacities. But it seems to me that it doesn't fully address constitutive luck. Even the capacities the agent brings to the moment of decision are themselves shaped by factors beyond their control. One person's capacity for moral reasoning was cultivated by stable, loving parents; another's was stunted by neglect or abuse. Both exercise whatever capacities they have, but those capacities are radically unequal, and that inequality is not their fault.
Professor: Pereboom argues that, given our best understanding of the causal structure of the universe, no one is ever truly morally responsible in the basic {{b11}} sense. We may still have good reasons for practices of praise, blame, and punishment — forward-looking reasons related to consequences and rehabilitation — but the {{b12}} element, the idea that wrongdoers basically deserve to suffer, cannot be justified.
Student: Liberating, because hard incompatibilism offers the most principled basis for a compassionate system of criminal justice — one that rejects punishment as retribution and focuses on harm-reduction, rehabilitation, and social protection. Alarming, because it seems to erode the self-conception that {{b13}} human dignity: the sense that I am the author of my own life, that my choices are genuinely mine, that what I make of myself reflects something real about me.
Professor: The most we can say, perhaps, is what Gary Watson has suggested: that responsibility is not a metaphysical property we either have or lack entirely, but a {{b14}} that varies with the degree to which an agent's action expresses their {{b15}} commitments — their deepest concerns and values — regardless of how those commitments were formed. We hold people responsible, on this view, not because they could have done otherwise in some absolute sense, but because their action expressed something about who they are. And who they are, however formed, is genuinely them.

blanks.explanation: Nagel calls the phenomenon 'moral luck' — when moral judgements are affected by factors outside one's control.
The 'control principle' states that responsibility requires control — only what you control can ground blame.
'Circumstantial luck' — luck in the situations one faces — is one of Nagel's four types.
'Constitutive luck' — luck in who one is, one's character and temperament.
Consistently applied, the control principle would 'dissolve' moral responsibility — make it disappear entirely.
'Vertiginous' — causing intellectual vertigo, a dizzying sense of disorientation.
Strawson's 'reactive attitudes' — resentment, indignation, gratitude — presuppose moral responsibility.
Wolf's 'sanity condition' requires the agent to have the psychological integrity needed for self-authorship.
'Self-authorship' — the sense of being the genuine author of one's own choices and life.
The capacities at the moment of action have 'uncontrollable antecedents' — prior causes the agent did not choose.
Pereboom denies responsibility in the 'basic desert sense' — the sense that wrongdoers deserve to suffer.
The 'retributive element' holds that punishment is deserved — not merely useful for deterrence or rehabilitation.
This self-conception 'underpins' human dignity — provides its foundation.
Watson views responsibility as a 'gradient' — a sliding scale rather than a binary property.
'Evaluative commitments' — the values and concerns that reflect what an agent most deeply cares about.
